scenarios.task: Vendor sent us their SaaS MSA. The limitation of liability says: 'Neither party's aggregate liability shall exceed the fees paid in the twelve (12) months preceding the claim. Neither party shall be liable for any indirect, incidental, or consequential damages. The foregoing limitations shall not apply to Customer's breach of Sections 4 (Payment) or 9 (Confidentiality).' We're the customer, handling PII in their system. Review this clause.
criteria: Maps each carve-out to the party that practically triggers it, concluding the mutuality is form-only (payment and confidentiality are customer-side breaches; vendor failure modes stay capped)
Proposes a data-breach/data-protection super-cap with a specific multiplier (e.g. 2-3x fees) or an uncapped data carve-out, given PII processing
Explains that the consequential-damages waiver combined with the cap bars the real losses of a data incident (notification costs, lost business) unless carved back
Provides actual proposed redline language or a numbered fallback ladder — critique alone does not satisfy this
Reasons from a concrete failure scenario (vendor breach exposing PII) rather than abstract fairness of the clause

scenarios.task: We're signing a $9k/year tool subscription and separately a $400k/year data processing agreement with a new vendor this month. Our legal budget is tight. How much review effort should each get, and what would you actually look at in each?
criteria: Explicitly tiers review depth by value and data sensitivity, stating the $9k tool gets minutes of targeted review, not a markup
For the $9k tool: names the auto-renewal notice window as the specific trap to check, plus at most 2-3 other quick checks
For the $400k data agreement: names at least four specific clause families (limitation of liability, indemnity, data protection/security, termination and data return, audit rights)
Recommends calendaring/diarying the renewal notice deadline as an operational step, not just 'be aware of it'
Frames over-lawyering the small contract as a real cost of legal time better spent on the $400k risk

scenarios.task: Our MSA says the order form controls over the MSA on conflict, and the vendor's order form incorporates their 'Service Terms available at [URL] as updated from time to time.' Procurement says this is standard boilerplate and wants to sign today. Anything to worry about?
criteria: States explicitly that 'as updated from time to time' grants the vendor a unilateral right to amend the deal after signature
Traces the precedence chain (order form over MSA; order form incorporates URL terms) to conclude the URL terms can override the negotiated MSA
Proposes a concrete freeze mechanism: attach a dated copy as an exhibit, or require notice-and-consent for changes to the URL terms
Rejects 'standard boilerplate' as a reason to sign while keeping the response proportionate to deal size
Advises archiving a dated copy of the current URL terms today regardless of negotiation outcome
